channel.title: Matthew Brensilver Dharma Talks
channel.description: A merged podcast feed of Matthew Brensilver Dharma talks from AudioDharma and Dharma Seed.
channel.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
channel.itunes:summary: A merged podcast feed of Matthew Brensilver Dharma talks from AudioDharma and Dharma Seed.

item.title: Dharmette: Insight & Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how insight and love are not separate paths, but mutually reinforcing responses to lived experience. He argues that when impermanence, unsatisfactoriness, and not-self are felt deeply rather than understood abstractly, they soften the heart and make care, non-violence, and forgiveness more natural. The talk moves from a question about wisdom and compassion into a grounded reflection on grief, selfing, and the relief of non-ownership.
Chapters: 00:17 Insight and love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25381/?t=17 02:01 Wisdom from ordinariness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25381/?t=121 03:27 Gunaratana on insight - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25381/?t=207 04:55 Heartbreak before freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25381/?t=295 06:23 Dukkha softens the heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25381/?t=383 08:00 Facing impermanence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25381/?t=480 10:38 Letting self relax - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25381/?t=638 13:34 Empty center, open heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25381/?t=814
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Very brief overview of Shinzen Young’s approach
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver gives a concise overview of Shinzen Young’s approach to mindfulness, treating it less as a vague quality than as a workable training map. He breaks the method into three interlocking skills—concentration, sensory clarity, and equanimity—then shows how that framework leads to three aims for practice, three sensory domains, and three basic modes of meditation. The talk is brief, practical, and reflective, with a few comments on why this system has stayed influential in his own teaching.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the sketch - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25294/?t=0 01:24 What mindfulness means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25294/?t=84 02:13 Three skills together - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25294/?t=133 03:50 The three skills unpacked - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25294/?t=230 07:36 Why practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25294/?t=456 10:22 Where to practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25294/?t=622 12:23 Three practice modes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25294/?t=743 15:20 Closing invitation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25294/?t=920
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25294

item.title: Dharmette: Ajahn Chah's Advice to Woman on Deathbed
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins by naming his own difficulty finding something to say in a time marked by war, anger, and helplessness, then turns to a remembered teaching from Ajahn Chah as a steadier response. The core of the talk is a deathbed instruction on letting go, seeing body and mind as not-self, and taking awareness and the breath as refuge. Matthew closes by reflecting that these words do not answer everything, but they can still matter as a necessary practice and a kind of love without control.
Chapters: 00:11 Searching for a talk - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25235/?t=11 02:53 Turning to a master - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25235/?t=173 03:57 Letting body and world go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25235/?t=237 06:06 Awareness as refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25235/?t=366 07:57 No self, no attachment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25235/?t=477 09:52 A gift of Dhamma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25235/?t=592 11:41 What words cannot do - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25235/?t=701
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/25235

item.title: Working with Self-Hatred
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver responds to a question about self-hatred and self-compassion by gently questioning the assumptions underneath both. He suggests looking for the functions self-hate serves, staying with suffering long enough to see it clearly, and trusting that deeper change may come through love, not force. The talk moves from practical inquiry into a broader reflection on faith, conditioning, retreat, and the ways deep feeling can open a different relationship to the self.

item.title: A Meaningful Dharma Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a quieter promise of Dharma practice: not only greater happiness, but a life that becomes more meaningful. He traces how the mind keeps making meaning, how practice loosens that habit, and how ethical conduct, vow, and lineage can build a more grounded sense of purpose. The talk moves from a research definition of meaning to personal examples, then toward compassion, non-regret, and the consolation of being part of a long stream of wisdom and love.
Chapters: 00:22 The promise of practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=22 01:18 Meaning and its making - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=78 03:31 What meaning links - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=211 05:04 A personal turn - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=304 05:59 Drama, culture, and emptiness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=359 08:58 Meaning from within - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=538 10:29 Practice and purpose - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=629 12:23 Vow and non-regret - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=743 15:04 Lineage and consolation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25073/?t=904

item.title: Dharmette: Meditation is so many things
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on meditation as something much larger than attention training: a way of resting, digesting pain, cultivating gratitude, and learning to trust experience as it changes. He moves through peace, suffering, goodness, samadhi, letting go, and the question of what it means to arrive in one’s life. The talk stays practical and restrained, while widening the frame from daily practice to mortality and completion.

item.title: Dharmette: Understanding, Forgiveness & Unconsciousness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver responds to a question about remorse after having done serious harm, especially the pain of not being able to make a coherent story about how it happened. He explores the limits of self-understanding, the role of unconscious forces, and how forgiveness can coexist with grief even when full explanation never arrives. The talk moves toward a gentler view of divided motivation, not-self, and the possibility of taking responsibility without disowning the parts of ourselves we do not fully know.
Chapters: 00:12 The Question of Remorse - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24994/?t=12 02:09 Witnessing Without Explanation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24994/?t=129 04:04 Seeking Forgiveness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24994/?t=244 05:55 Reasons and Causes - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24994/?t=355 08:34 The Unconscious at Work - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24994/?t=514 09:44 Divided Selves - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24994/?t=584 12:04 Pleasure and Renunciation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24994/?t=724 13:34 Love and Heartbreak - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24994/?t=814
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Self, Self-Love, Selflessness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how insight practice moves between simple clarity and complex psychological terrain, using self, self-love, and selflessness as a dialectic rather than a single answer. He argues that stability and repetition are not obstacles to awakening but the conditions that let disorientation, insight, and relinquishment be integrated safely. Along the way he draws on retreat life, neuroscience, and teachers such as Manindraji, Jack Engler, Ajahn Sucitto, and Thomas Metzinger to show why a workable sense of self can support deeper letting go.
Chapters: 00:09 Simplicity and complexity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=9 01:16 The dharma as dialectic - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=76 04:00 Stability and disorientation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=240 08:18 What self means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=498 13:21 Learning readiness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=801 20:47 Brain balance and retreat - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=1247 25:21 A self that can hold - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=1521 30:13 Self-love and ownership - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=1813 37:28 Unbinding and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-25024/?t=2248

item.title: Dharmette: Question on Social Anxiety
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver responds to a question about social anxiety by beginning with acceptance rather than self-improvement. He moves from non-resistance and feeling the pain directly into a careful look at shame, objectification, and the fear of being seen by others. The talk closes by turning toward anatta, suggesting that the deepest relief may come through loosening the sense of a fixed self rather than defending it.

item.title: Dharmette: How We Incarnate in Different Contexts
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses a line from Ajahn Chah’s aspiration verses to explore how we “incarnate” differently from moment to moment and in different roles. He moves from the apparent stability of personality to the subtler ways sankharas, emotion, and social context shape the self we become in each situation. The talk closes with practical questions for noticing what gets amplified, what gets forgotten, and how Dharma is present or distant in different settings.

item.title: Dharmette: Tranquility, View, Truth
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores a simple but demanding question: in what state do we see most clearly? Drawing on the Buddha’s link between truth and freedom, he argues that clear perception depends on bodily balance, calm, and a mind less crowded by fear, arousal, and narrative certainty. The talk moves from the way mood shapes view to the quieter, more reliable seeing that can arise in tranquility, while still recognizing dukkha and the distortions of greed and hatred.

item.title: Dharmette: Orienting to Goodness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on what it means to orient toward goodness when suffering, fear, and political division can make love seem inadequate or naive. He argues that goodness is not a luxury but something the mind and the world both need, and he connects that claim to a recent peace walk, to the Dalai Lama’s emphasis on compassion as necessity, and to Samuel Bagg’s writing on epistemic trust and democracy. The talk moves from a personal and moral case for goodness into a practical account of how love softens identity, supports solidarity, and makes genuine listening possible.

item.title: Orienting and ruling out the alternatives to love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens by orienting the retreat and making a case for wholehearted participation, including the value—and limits—of online practice. From there he turns to loving-kindness as a way of ruling out hate and apathy, then explores why those alternatives can feel psychologically useful before showing how compassion, rest, and equanimity can make love sustainable. The talk moves into a guided settling period where he invites listeners to sense what kind of love is needed in the moment, and to rest without slipping into detachment.

item.title: Love, Grief and Inconsolability
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on grief, love, and mortality by moving between Buddhist impermanence teachings, a deathbed instruction from Ajahn Chah, and his own questions about fear and control. He considers what Dharma can and cannot heal, especially when suffering remains irreducible or inconsolable. The talk turns toward a quieter freedom: love that is less entangled with outcome, more willing to simply illuminate.

item.title: Functions of heart practice: cultivation, concentration, purification
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a talk and guided practice on metta as something flexible rather than fixed: a way of meeting the truth of the moment with whatever form of care is actually available. He frames heart practice through three functions—cultivation, concentration, and purification—and emphasizes that the work may look like resting in love, focusing on a phrase, or allowing buried pain to surface. The guided section then moves from settling the body into a slower, patient practice of letting time, words, and goodwill do their work.

item.title: Anatta as a cause and effect of freedom
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the relationship between not-self and freedom by asking a practical question: does insight into anatta cause liberation, or does it emerge as a result of the path? He uses the idea of causality to distinguish correlation from cause, then moves through how selfing, shame, suffering, ethics, love, humility, and psychological healing gradually loosen identification. The talk ends by cautioning against treating isolated peak experiences as the whole path, emphasizing a holistic practice that can make freedom and not-self mutually shape one another.

item.title: Spiritual vs Psychological Health
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the relationship between psychological health and spiritual awakening by laying out four possible ways to understand their connection: separation, continuity, sequence, and mutual threading. He then argues for a more nuanced view, where Dharma both heals psychological pain and reveals deeper layers of conditioning that cannot be reduced to therapy alone. The talk moves from theory into practice-level questions about self-love, disidentification, and why spiritual work often brings old wounds into sharper relief.

item.title: Wise Intention
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores wise intention as something more gradual and relational than a single decision. After a long guided sit, he turns to the gap between intention and behavior, the role of habit, and why sincere practice is less about self-improvement than about letting go of grasping. The talk ends with a wide-ranging dialogue on surrender, addiction, sincerity, and how renunciation, metta, and harmlessness shape daily life.
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the posture - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=0 07:53 Attending with gentleness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=473 15:21 Breath as the anchor - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=921 23:06 Sincerity over striving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=1386 38:29 Habits and values - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=2309 52:36 Intention and behavior - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=3156 01:05:51 Three wise intentions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=3951 01:15:26 Questions on consequences - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=4526 01:21:29 Willingness and metta - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-94445/?t=4889

After a long guided sit, he turns to the gap between intention and behavior, the role of habit, and why sincere practice is less about self-improvement than about letting go of grasping. The talk ends with a wide-ranging dialogue on surrender, addiction, sincerity, and how renunciation, metta, and harmlessness shape daily life.

item.title: TS Eliot and the Mystical Dimension of Buddhism
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the mystical dimension of Buddhism without romanticizing mystical experience. Using William James, T.S. Eliot, Joseph Goldstein, and a short canonical citation, he traces a path of ignorance, dispossession, non-ownership, and relinquishing views. The talk stays close to practice: ordinary life, egoic footing, and the quiet relief that can come from not knowing.

item.title: Dharmette: Very Condensed Overview of the Path
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a very condensed map of how major Dharma teachings fit together, moving from the problem of seeking peace through possession to the diagnosis of dukkha, anicca, and anatta. He then sketches how practice supports letting go through renunciation, mettā, and harmlessness, and how wise view and ethical discipline stabilize the path. The talk closes by showing how mindfulness and equanimity de-reify experience without becoming cold or abstract, ending with a note that this kind of dissolving is inseparable from love.

item.title: Spiritual Friendship (Berkeley Satellite Daylong)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on spiritual friendship as something that both supports and grows out of practice, beginning with the mind’s social permeability and the need for good influences. He moves through belonging, early dependence, isolation, truthfulness, and the relief of being known without performance. Near the end, he turns to play, creativity, and “feeling real” as ways friendship softens self-consciousness and helps us return to ordinary goodness.

item.title: Dharmette: Rivers are just rivers
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses Dogen’s image of mountains and rivers to trace a practice arc from ordinary certainty, through destabilizing insight, to a more mature ordinariness. He moves from the mind’s assumptions and a brief neuroscience aside into meditation as a kind of reversal of our usual attentional habits, where the self feels less fixed and experience more fluid. The talk ends by softening the apparent paradox: awakening does not replace ordinary life so much as make love and intimacy more visible within it.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: The What and How of Investigation
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores investigation as a practice of attention rather than a search for quick answers. He questions the urge to turn practice into an algorithm, distinguishes genuine inquiry from compulsive thinking, and shows how insight often appears when the mind relaxes its demand to know. The talk closes by framing dhammavichaya as a way of looking that avoids premature story-making and stays open to what is not yet clear.

item.title: Dharmette: Empathic Resonance
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores empathy as something that begins with close attention to one’s own mind and then extends outward without collapsing self and other. He distinguishes emotional resonance from perspective-taking, emphasizes humility and nonprojective listening, and returns several times to the need for the “as-if” condition in compassionate contact. The talk moves from mindfulness as self-directed empathy to a more careful account of calibrated, boundaried presence in healing relationships.

item.title: Dharmette: attention, awareness, mindfulness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver distinguishes attention from awareness and mindfulness, arguing that practice is not just about focusing on an object but also noticing the background quality of the mind that knows it. He moves from a simple model of spotlighted attention to a broader account of receptive awareness, relaxation, and the way craving can quietly shape what seems like curiosity. The talk ends by reframing mindfulness as a subtle freedom: seeing clinging as an object rather than obeying it.

item.title: Living Alongside Questions (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores what it means to live with impermanence, uncertainty, and unfinished questions rather than trying to force life into certainty. He moves from the mind’s habit of assuming continuity to the way fixed views can harden into fear and harm, then into a gentler practice of opening to love, understanding, equanimity, and the limits of control. The talk closes by looking honestly at recurring patterns, suffering that returns over decades, and the possibility of staying open anyway.
Chapters: 00:01 Settling into the room - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=1 01:34 The illusion of permanence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=94 05:15 Impermanence and uncertainty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=315 07:30 Views and certainty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=450 10:11 A life with questions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=611 12:29 Two koans of practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=749 16:45 Love as understanding - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=1005 22:45 The circle of concern - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=1365 27:22 Compassion and equanimity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93338/?t=1642

item.title: Reflections on Time in Dharma: Surrendering to Time, Healing Time, Not-Making Time (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on time as a central pressure in practice: too much time, too little time, and the urge to fill it. From there he moves through boredom, grief, retreat structure, samadhi, impermanence, memory, and self-view, gradually shifting time from something that burdens the heart to something that can soften it. The talk closes by suggesting that when the churning of becoming quiets, there is a kind of refuge in timelessness and simple presence.

item.title: Dharmette: PR Campaign for Non-Hatred
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on what part of the Dharma could have public reach in a time shaped by anger and polarization. He argues that non-hatred may be the most realistic and useful teaching to broadcast, distinguishing it from love while grounding it in bodily, relational, and canonical evidence. The talk closes with a William Stafford poem that deepens the call for mutual recognition and clear signals in dark times.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Context - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24005/?t=12 00:59 Dharma And Public Reach - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24005/?t=59 03:49 Choosing Non-Hatred - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24005/?t=229 05:19 The Cost Of Hatred - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24005/?t=319 09:03 Non-Hatred In Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24005/?t=543 10:55 Guard The Senses - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24005/?t=655 12:44 Stafford's Warning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-24005/?t=764
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: 'Protect Empathy at All Costs and Live Groovy Lives'
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens with a reflection on Jim Jarmusch and a remark about art changing the world by opening people to one another. From there he develops a quiet argument that Dharma, sangha, and practice can be understood as forms of cultivating beauty and protecting empathy, both inwardly and socially. A personal story about Joe Strummer becomes the talk’s central refrain: protect empathy at all costs and live groovy lives.
Chapters: 00:12 Art Opens the Heart - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23938/?t=12 01:46 Strummer's Charge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23938/?t=106 03:08 Dharma as Art - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23938/?t=188 04:17 Beauty and Sangha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23938/?t=257 05:40 Abiding in What We Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23938/?t=340 07:31 Expanding the Circle - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23938/?t=451 09:40 Seeing Others Clearly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23938/?t=580 11:44 A Lineage of Care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23938/?t=704

item.title: Dharma Practice and the Cultivation of Power
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins by acknowledging the usual Dharma emphasis on softening, vulnerability, surrender, and the limits of what our love can reach. He then pivots to a more unusual framing: that the path also cultivates power — the strength to face truth, tolerate pain, understand causality, and become less dependent on conditions. The talk closes by contrasting this inner power with fear, delusion, and self-view, and by suggesting that Dharma can offer a steadier happiness that is less fragile and more trustworthy.
Chapters: 00:12 Softening and helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23888/?t=12 02:55 A different kind of power - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23888/?t=175 05:00 Truth requires strength - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23888/?t=300 06:56 Causality and relinquishment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23888/?t=416 09:01 Independent in the Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23888/?t=541 10:27 Refusing cultural confusion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23888/?t=627 12:23 Power and trust - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23888/?t=743
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: The Circuitous Journey to Presence through Past and Future
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores why “be present” is not a simple instruction but the fruit of a long process of seeing through memory, anticipation, and the habits that organize selfhood. He moves from humor and personal recollection into a careful argument about pain, grief, and the way mindfulness makes the past less personal and the future less compelling. The talk closes by returning to time, fear, and freedom, with a poetic coda from Bob Dylan.

item.title: Active and Receptive Dimensions of Practice
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames practice as a balance between doing and receiving, using the Middle Path to explore how attention, patience, and renunciation each need their counterpart. He draws on William James, Ajahn Sucitto, Thich Nhat Hanh, Adler, and Mahasi-style effort language to show both the usefulness and the limits of willpower in Dharma practice. The talk gradually moves toward a quieter trust: that healing depends on conditions, that time can become an ally, and that practice often works by softening rather than forcing.

item.title: Attention Models the Object, Mindfulness Models the Attention
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins with a few words about listening to Dharma talks and the mood of retreat, then turns to the first-day forces that often dominate practice: hindrances, discomfort, and the effort to stay simple with unpleasant experience. From there he builds a careful distinction between attention and mindfulness, using the Satipatthana Sutta, the Dhammapada, and a modern philosopher’s account of mind-wandering to show how perception, identification, and self-modeling shift from moment to moment. The talk closes with a practical emphasis on allowing experience without trying to improve it, and on recognizing that real mindfulness includes awareness of how attention itself is functioning.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and retreat frame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93047/?t=0 01:51 The first-day hindrances - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93047/?t=111 05:59 Mindfulness in the sutta - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93047/?t=359 08:03 Mind-made experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93047/?t=483 12:28 Mind-wandering and self-models - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93047/?t=748 24:08 What attention does - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93047/?t=1448 29:40 Control versus connection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93047/?t=1780 34:37 Allowing instead of improving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-93047/?t=2077

item.title: Dharmette: Opportunities and Complexities of Speaking about Awakening
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on why teachers may or may not speak explicitly about awakening, using personal anecdotes and several Dharma and psychology references to show both the value and the risks of doing so. He moves from the Buddha’s direct language about liberation, to the different needs of group and one-on-one teaching, and then to the tension between urgency, patience, and ordinary psychological healing. The talk ends with a careful reminder that awakening is hard to describe without turning it into another project of the self.
Chapters: 00:18 A Pep Talk Gone Wrong - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=18 01:13 Freedom Beyond Conditions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=73 02:49 The Buddha's Straight Talk - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=169 04:05 Group Context Matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=245 05:20 Psychological Shifts - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=320 07:01 Urgency and Patience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=421 08:44 Many Fruits of Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=524 10:39 The Self's Difficulties - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=639 13:12 Complicated Cause and Effect - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23819/?t=792

item.title: Dharmette: Uprooting vs Allowing
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver answers a question about when to allow experience and when to actively work with unwholesome mind states. He emphasizes that practice is not a fixed hierarchy of techniques, but a matter of discernment, training, and what actually reduces suffering. The talk moves from restraint and equanimity to more active cognitive methods, then into listening for the deeper need beneath jealousy, anger, fear, and self-protection.
Chapters: 00:12 The question of technique - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23722/?t=12 01:43 Don't fetishize technique - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23722/?t=103 02:30 Training before crisis - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23722/?t=150 04:23 Equanimity and aversion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23722/?t=263 05:58 Marinating versus being with - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23722/?t=358 07:49 Active redirection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23722/?t=469 10:12 Listen for the signal - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23722/?t=612 12:36 Self, fear, and peace - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23722/?t=756

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (5of5); The Parami of Energy
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on energy as a central part of practice, starting with how easily the mind defaults to the path of least resistance. He connects effort to the Buddha’s teaching in MN 26, then turns to retreat dynamics, the Tibetan four thoughts, and the difference between striving and a more love-based willingness. The talk closes by asking what effort looks like when it is no longer organized around self-assertion.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening on effort - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23688/?t=0 02:02 Hard to see the Dhamma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23688/?t=122 04:17 Against default programming - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23688/?t=257 05:52 When energy rises and falls - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23688/?t=352 07:39 Four reflections - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23688/?t=459 10:30 Karma and samsara - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23688/?t=630 11:57 Effort without self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23688/?t=717 13:11 Closing blessing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23688/?t=791
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (4of5); The Parami of Metta
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on metta as a kind of love that is joined with wisdom, not sentimentality. He moves from a playful critique of Hallmark-style love into a broader account of how kindness steadies us, protects us from hatred, and helps us meet reality with courage. The talk closes by framing loving-kindness as something we discover in lived experience and in the nervous system, not as an idea to admire from afar.

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (3of5); Parami of Wisdom
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice through a simple ladder: data, information, knowledge, and wisdom. He begins with direct experience and the shock of actually observing the mind, then shows how patterns, stories, and causality make practice intelligible. The talk closes by asking how wisdom can help us predict what truly supports happiness, reduces suffering, and serves others.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: NIsargadatta, Nothing, Everything, Wisdom, Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins with a Nisargadatta quote and uses it to frame meditation as a lived tension between emptiness and boundless love. He then moves through how practice can thin out selfing, feeling tone, and the sense of an observer, before turning to the tenderness that appears when self-clinging loosens. The talk closes with a Shinzen Young passage that gathers the talk into a patient, alternating recognition of nothingness and reified experience.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening quote - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=12 01:26 Two poles of practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=86 02:38 Precise experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=158 04:11 Phenomena dissolving - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=251 06:21 Self becomes space - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=381 08:03 Desert of the real - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=483 09:59 Love after emptiness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=599 12:38 Seeking settles - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=758 13:38 Shinzen's summary - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23668/?t=818

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (2of5); The Parami of Patience
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses patient, practical examples to explore the parami of patience as something more than waiting: a steadiness that can hold suffering without immediate reaction. He moves from a sweat-lodge story and Thich Nhat Hanh’s embodied presence into a wider teaching on how practice changes us gradually, often beneath awareness. The talk closes by reframing meditation, self-kindness, and commitment as ways of slowly learning to love something new.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and framing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=0 00:38 The hard path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=38 01:22 Thich Nhat Hanh's example - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=82 03:23 Patience and suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=203 05:52 Gradual cultivation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=352 07:03 Holding the heart still - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=423 08:07 States become traits - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=487 11:58 Learning new love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=718 15:29 Closing blessing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23657/?t=929

item.title: Dharmette: Wholesome Qualities of Mind (1 of 5): The Parami of Truthfulness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on truthfulness as a foundational virtue in the Dharma path, moving from the parami tradition into personal memory, clinical insight, and social critique. He traces how honesty supports both inner sanity and mutual understanding, especially when deception and self-deception distort perception. The talk closes by turning truthfulness into a quiet practice of listening, resisting bad faith, and learning to tolerate what is real.

item.title: Reflections on Freedom
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a long, steady reflection on freedom as something discovered through relaxation, equanimity, and the softening of self-clinging. The talk moves from guided practice into a teaching on why the path is less about forcing progress and more about allowing life to arise and pass without resistance. He ends by connecting freedom with love, satisfaction, and the end of chasing an imagined life.

item.title: The Circuitous Journey to Presence through Past and Future
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on why “being present” can feel less like a choice than the result of long training. He explores how memory, regret, fear, and future-orientation keep pulling the mind away from refuge, then argues that practice works gradually by making the past less personal and the future less threatening. Along the way he brings in Thich Nhat Hanh, Ajahn Sucitto, Leonard Cohen, Zadie Smith, and Bob Dylan to show how Dharma moves through time rather than bypassing it.
Chapters: 00:00 What presence means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=0 01:50 Thich Nhat Hanh's influence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=110 04:03 Presence as fruit - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=243 07:10 Dharma and conditionality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=430 10:06 Backlog of the past - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=606 13:34 Self and opacity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=814 18:04 Grief loosens shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=1084 25:22 The urge to fill time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=1522 31:49 Futurelessness and refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23629/?t=1909

item.title: Impressionistic v. Precise Meditation Instructions
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the tension between impressionistic and highly precise meditation instruction, using stories from Ajahn Chah, Shinzen Young, and Ajahn Sucitto to think aloud about how Dharma is taught and learned. He then turns to a more structured practice map, outlining mindfulness as concentration, clarity, and equanimity, and applying it to the felt experience of images, inner speech, and emotion. The talk moves from a broad meditation on pedagogy into a practical framework for noticing experience with enough precision to be useful, but without losing the larger view.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and setting - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=0 01:00 Watching the light - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=60 03:36 Beyond judging talks - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=216 06:45 Impressionistic or precise - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=405 13:57 Benefits and limits - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=837 18:09 Change of direction - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=1089 20:27 Threefold mindfulness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=1227 23:54 Core subjective triad - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=1434 33:54 Noting and closing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23624/?t=2034

Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/23624
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the tension between impressionistic and highly precise meditation instruction, using stories from Ajahn Chah, Shinzen Young, and Ajahn Sucitto to think aloud about how Dharma is taught and learned. He then turns to a more structured practice map, outlining mindfulness as concentration, clarity, and equanimity, and applying it to the felt experience of images, inner speech, and emotion. The talk moves from a broad meditation on pedagogy into a practical framework for noticing experience with enough precision to be useful, but without losing the larger view.

item.title: Dharmette: Fear, Love and Political Chaos
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how fear, love, and political turmoil shape ethical practice, without offering a tidy answer. He treats love as a living koan: something to investigate in relation to suffering, balance, helplessness, and the wish to act without becoming controlled by fear or moralism. The talk moves toward a quieter, more radical compassion shaped by impermanence, mortality, and the question of what it means to care when we cannot fix anything.

item.title: Dharmette: Curiosity and Goodness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how the Dharma’s goodness becomes visible over time, especially when practice is shaped less by striving and more by genuine curiosity. He contrasts control with open interest, then turns to stubborn “karmic knots” as places where acceptance, patience, and relationship matter more than technique. The talk closes by describing how interest and love can feed one another until practice feels less like effort and more like a natural unfolding.

item.title: Dharmette: Death at the Center of Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on mortality as something woven into the present rather than a distant ending. Moving from William James and Kafka to Hegel, Thich Nhat Hanh, and the five remembrances, he asks how finitude shapes meaning, practice, and love. The talk settles into a practical question: if death is already at the center of life, what becomes possible when we are a little less afraid?
Chapters: 00:18 From golf to mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=18 00:54 Death at the core - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=54 02:23 Living with finitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=143 03:59 Eternity and meaning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=239 05:58 Inter-being and loss - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=358 06:56 The deathless and refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=416 08:33 Five remembrances - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=513 09:42 Uncertainty at the center - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=582 11:19 Selfing and freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23460/?t=679

item.title: Dharmette: Unquenchability and Questions of Meaning
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens with a light, unexpected detour through golf and a recent quote from Scottie Scheffler, then uses it to explore a serious question: why accomplishment so often fails to satisfy. From there he turns to dukkha as not only suffering but also the heart’s unquenchability, asking what is actually worth wanting and why some questions cannot be answered in words. He closes by drawing on Rilke and on Dharma practice itself to suggest that the real answer is lived, trained, and gradually embodied.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening with golf - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23422/?t=12 02:08 Scheffler asks why - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23422/?t=128 05:40 More than suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23422/?t=340 07:27 What is worth wanting - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23422/?t=447 09:04 Rilke and the questions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23422/?t=544 09:39 Answered through training - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23422/?t=579 11:27 Doubt dissolves - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23422/?t=687
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Unconscious Acting-Out
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on unconscious acting-out as a limit to the familiar story of a unified, knowable self. Using Freud, Dan Dennett, and Yogacara’s storehouse consciousness, he shows how habits can leak through in ways we do not intend, often bringing shame and a need for repair. The talk turns toward investigation, self-knowledge, and the role of other people in helping us meet what is hidden without collapsing into self-judgment.

item.title: Morning Instruction: Reviewing the Factors of Enlightenment
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a morning instruction on the factors of enlightenment as a practical way to meet experience with less strain and more trust. He moves from “enoughness” and basic okay-ness into mindfulness, investigation, energy, rapture, tranquility, and samadhi, then widens into equanimity and a quieter relationship to future-thinking. The talk closes by linking emptiness with love, with a brief reference to Shinzen Young’s phrase about ordinary life and Dharma life.

item.title: Samadhi
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores samadhi as something that cannot be forced, but arises from causes and conditions: steadiness, letting go, and a quieted relationship to experience. He moves from attention and classical Buddhist categories into a practical account of how concentration softens fragmentation, reduces strategizing, and makes the mind more receptive to learning. Along the way he contrasts samadhi with craving, self-management, and the urge to keep narrating and controlling life.

item.title: Energetic Vigor, Self-View & Rapturous Interest
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on energetic vigor as something that needs a clear, personal purpose, not borrowed ideals or sheer willpower. He moves from why we practice, to the way conditioning and self-view shape effort, and then to the difference between striving and the quieter effortlessness of genuine interest. The talk closes by distinguishing disciplined energy from performance, and by pointing toward pīti as a more natural, rapturous way of engaging practice.

item.title: Morning Instruction: Equanimity precedes Mindfulness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet morning instruction centered on the idea that equanimity comes before mindfulness: first loosening the habit of control, then becoming available to the present moment. He returns again and again to breathing, forgiveness of imperfection, and the way awareness can be simple, effortless, and nonjudgmental. The talk gradually moves from instruction to a gentle invitation to notice the beginning and ending of each breath, and to stay open even when experience includes helplessness, fear, or aversion.

item.title: Sati
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a careful talk on sati, or mindfulness, as more than a bare attentional skill. He moves from the Dharma’s emphasis on letting go toward the fuller claim that awakening also includes what is cultivated, sustained, and taken up as a form of peace. Along the way he connects mindfulness with the seven factors of awakening, mind-wandering, reactivity, and the subtle ways identification with thought narrows experience.

item.title: Dharmette: Moral Vandalism, Anger and Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver begins with a somatic story about a meditation injury and a bodyworker’s question, using it to explore how unfinished fight energy can live in the body. From there he turns to the taboo around anger in Dharma circles, distinguishing its delusion from whatever wisdom it may still contain, then asks how moral outrage can be expressed without harm. The talk moves through a Buddhist caution about anger, a reflection on Martin Luther King Jr.’s nonviolence, and ends with King’s definition of agape as active, redemptive goodwill rather than sentimentality.
Chapters: 00:09 Somatic starting point - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23190/?t=9 02:43 The taboo of anger - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23190/?t=163 04:55 Danger and restraint - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23190/?t=295 06:04 Wisdom inside anger - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23190/?t=364 09:18 Moral outrage needs a path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23190/?t=558 10:51 King as model - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23190/?t=651 13:25 Agape, not sentiment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23190/?t=805 15:28 Closing encouragement - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23190/?t=928

item.title: Beyond Judging, Comparing and Fixing: Fixing
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the hidden drive to fix, improve, and become somebody better, and how that impulse shapes meditation, self-story, and even our relationship to pain. He distinguishes necessary practical adjustment from compulsive fixing, then slowly turns the talk toward receptivity, equanimity, and a non-gaining way of practicing. The talk closes by tying this stance to healing the past, meeting uncertainty, and letting life be enough as it is.
Chapters: 00:00 One Fidget Away - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23104/?t=0 02:55 The Fixing Impulse - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23104/?t=175 08:14 Pain and Pressure - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23104/?t=494 13:33 Secret Motives - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23104/?t=813 19:40 Non-Gaining Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23104/?t=1180 25:35 Curiosity Over Outcome - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23104/?t=1535 31:25 Blessing the Past - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23104/?t=1885 37:03 Enough, Just This - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23104/?t=2223
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Beyond Judging Comparing and Fixing: Judging
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver examines the difference between judgment and discernment, beginning with mindfulness and the question of what it means to meet experience without extra defensiveness. He turns to the First Noble Truth, the two arrows, and the ways pain gets layered with aversion, self-protection, and story, then broadens the lens to anger, forgiveness, and self-judgment. The talk closes by reframing enlightenment as deep acceptance and self-love as a form of non-clinging rather than self-idealization.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=0 01:57 Mindfulness and Judgment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=117 04:05 Judgment Versus Discernment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=245 09:07 Learning Peace - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=547 15:44 The Two Arrows - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=944 18:59 Pain as Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=1139 24:15 Anger and Accountability - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=1455 27:16 Forgiveness and Equanimity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=1636 31:47 Self-Love and Freedom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23094/?t=1907

item.title: Dharmette: When Inspiritation to Practice is Lost
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver answers a question about what to do when inspiration to practice fades. He treats low motivation as something to understand rather than immediately fix, then explores how practice can stagnate when we ask it to do too much, stay centered on the self, or stand in for other needed forms of support. The talk moves toward a quieter counsel: remember why the path matters, lean on spiritual friendship and urgency, and sometimes rest in confusion until a direction appears.
Chapters: 00:30 Making Room for Low Inspiration - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23056/?t=30 02:43 Revisiting the Original Why - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23056/?t=163 04:38 What Dharma Can’t Do - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23056/?t=278 06:59 When Action Is Missing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23056/?t=419 08:44 Intrinsic Interest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23056/?t=524 10:27 Love for Others - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23056/?t=627 12:22 Urgency and the Four Thoughts - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23056/?t=742 13:56 Resting in Confusion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-23056/?t=836
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Sila Reflections
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on sila as more than ethical rule-following: a way of becoming a refuge, softening around suffering, and letting love and wisdom mature together. He moves through grief, humility, and the willingness to be wrong, then widens the lens to moral imagination, cultural conditioning, and the limits of ego. The talk ends by turning back to completion, suggesting that practice is learning to die before we die and to let this life be enough.

item.title: Fear, Thinking, Safety and Samadhi (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how fear, selfing, and compulsive thinking arise together as ways the mind tries to stay safe. He moves from early attachment and the formation of identity into the defensive uses of language, then toward samadhi as a more integrated, less ambivalent way of abiding. The talk gradually turns from analysis to trust: courage, faith, and a willingness to let silence, groundlessness, and even enjoyment be non-threatening.

item.title: Dharmette: 'A thing that masquerades as freedom of attention is on the rampage'
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on attention as something deeply tied to love, desire, and ethical life, while arguing that modern technological frenzy distorts all three. He quotes a 2019 Friends of Attention manifesto, then moves through Rilke, Naomi Shihab Nye, and Simone Weil to show how slowing down and staying with experience can loosen craving and hatred. The talk settles into a quiet claim: wise attention is not a technique for control, but a form of prayer that lets the soul be drawn toward the good.
Chapters: 00:19 Attention and love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=19 01:31 The manifesto's warning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=91 02:56 True freedom of attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=176 04:29 Craving tightened - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=269 06:08 Rilke and slowing down - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=368 07:58 Depth over surface - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=478 10:28 Attention as prayer - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=628 12:11 Giving up hate - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=731
Source: AudioDharma

He quotes a 2019 Friends of Attention manifesto, then moves through Rilke, Naomi Shihab Nye, and Simone Weil to show how slowing down and staying with experience can loosen craving and hatred. The talk settles into a quiet claim: wise attention is not a technique for control, but a form of prayer that lets the soul be drawn toward the good.
Chapters: 00:19 Attention and love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=19 01:31 The manifesto's warning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=91 02:56 True freedom of attention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=176 04:29 Craving tightened - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=269 06:08 Rilke and slowing down - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=368 07:58 Depth over surface - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=478 10:28 Attention as prayer - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=628 12:11 Giving up hate - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22905/?t=731

item.title: Somebody, Nobody, Self-Love, Anatta
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on an old Jack Engler teaching about having to be “somebody before being nobody,” then uses that frame to clarify how insight practice depends on some stability rather than collapse into confusion. From there he turns to the language of self-love and anatta, arguing that self-love is better understood as forgiveness, non-clinging, and a first loosening of identification. The talk closes by linking this gentleness to a deeper metacognitive shift in which what once felt like “the place from which I see” becomes something seen.
Chapters: 00:15 Looking Back - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22861/?t=15 02:12 Somebody Before Nobody - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22861/?t=132 04:43 Language and Self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22861/?t=283 05:11 Self-Love as Forgiveness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22861/?t=311 07:45 Not-Self Is Flexibility - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22861/?t=465 09:59 The Self Model Opens - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22861/?t=599 12:27 Return to Innocence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22861/?t=747
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/22861

item.title: Dharmette: Pleasant and Unpleasant Dissolution
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores why dissolution can feel peaceful in one setting and terrifying in another, using emptiness, derealization, and death anxiety as a comparison. He brings in Nagarjuna, a recent study by Aviva Berkovich-Ohana and Stephen Fulder, and Stephen Levine to show how the Dharma changes the meaning of self-loss. The talk closes by returning to impermanence, grief, and the courage that practice can build around mortality.
Chapters: 00:17 A peaceful dissolution - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22801/?t=17 01:17 When emptiness unsettles - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22801/?t=77 02:34 Nagarjuna on emptiness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22801/?t=154 04:05 Why experiences diverge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22801/?t=245 06:08 Self as fear's effect - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22801/?t=368 08:20 Craving to become - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22801/?t=500 10:20 Impermanence at the center - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22801/?t=620 12:14 Grief and courage - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22801/?t=734
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Know Thyself, Release Thyself, Merging
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how meditation can become a quiet process of self-familiarization: seeing one’s tendencies clearly, loosening stale self-stories, and learning what is actually wanted beneath culture and habit. He moves from personality types and shame to embodiment, intimacy, and the way a less defended sense of self can make room for attunement, spontaneity, and even a kind of merging that does not erase boundaries. Several brief references to Winnicott, Thich Nhat Hanh, Octavio Paz, and others help him frame the talk around aliveness, understanding, and love that reveals freedom.
Chapters: 00:00 Know Thyself - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91357/?t=0 03:45 Habit Energies - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91357/?t=225 07:26 Seeing and Being Seen - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91357/?t=446 12:38 New Ways of Thinking - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91357/?t=758 19:11 Disidentifying From Self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91357/?t=1151 24:50 Embodiment and Aliveness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91357/?t=1490 32:42 Love Without Possession - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91357/?t=1962 40:54 Merging and Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91357/?t=2454
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Dukkha as Ambivalence in Relational Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores dukkha through the lens of ambivalence in love, friendship, family, and group life. He moves from comic and psychoanalytic references to a practical Buddhist account of equanimity: how to stay with mixed feeling, resist the urge to control, and distinguish care from clinging. The talk closes by returning to compassion as love in the face of helplessness, and to the difficulty of living with the imperfection of others without hardening or taking over.
Chapters: 00:00 A Comic Opening - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=0 02:31 Love and Lack - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=151 05:54 Pain and Disconnection - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=354 09:29 What Ambivalence Means - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=569 14:30 Freud and Mixed Feeling - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=870 17:51 Equanimity Widens - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=1071 24:57 Acceptance in Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=1497 29:01 Relationship and Dependence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=1741 39:44 Compassion Without Control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91355/?t=2384

item.title: Dharma Supports Relationships, Relationships Help Us Get Freer
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how Dharma practice can support relationships without turning them into projects, and how relationships can, in turn, reveal where we are still not free. He moves between personal stories, contemporary psychology, and Buddhist teaching to argue for love, ethical care, and clarity without promises that life will cooperate. The talk stays grounded in the tension between intimacy and freedom, ending with a quiet reminder that we need both a lighter touch and a wider view.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening cautions - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=12 01:36 Practice without bargaining - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=96 04:18 Conditions, not guarantees - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=258 06:27 Truth and relationship - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=387 09:09 Freedom and attachment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=549 13:25 Belonging and isolation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=805 18:52 Sangha and non-harming - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=1132 24:53 Love, clarity, and fog - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=1493 30:28 One foot out - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91353/?t=1828

item.title: Landing in the Breath
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet meditation on how the mind habitually turns present experience into a search for future safety, meaning, and self-confirmation. He begins with a body scan and then moves into a wider teaching on possibility, uncertainty, and the relief of returning to the actual breath. The latter part of the talk turns practical, with guidance for sitting and walking practice, including how to work with boredom, attention, and shifting anchors without forcing the mind.
Chapters: 00:00 Arriving in the body - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91352/?t=0 07:38 Possibility in experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91352/?t=458 11:31 Landing in the breath - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91352/?t=691 20:47 Loose ends and uncertainty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91352/?t=1247 33:19 Hope in this moment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91352/?t=1999 40:46 Not overreading retreat - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91352/?t=2446 43:06 Walking with ease - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91352/?t=2586 48:18 Boredom and fresh anchors - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-91352/?t=2898

item.title: Dharmette: Power and Safety
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the strange mix of power, responsibility, and humility that comes with Dharma life. Drawing on a story about Ajahn Chah and the Thai forest tradition, he warns against ego inflation in teaching, then turns to sila, feedback, and communal safety as practical protections. The talk moves toward a quiet but forceful conclusion: practice can deepen our power, but it should also make us safer, more transparent, and more able to serve as a refuge for others.
Chapters: 00:36 Why Power Matters - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22693/?t=36 01:35 Ajahn Chah's Warning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22693/?t=95 04:57 Ripples of Goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22693/?t=297 06:45 Dharma as Response - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22693/?t=405 08:22 Unpossessable Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22693/?t=502 10:52 Feedback and Safety - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22693/?t=652 12:07 Power and Prestige - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22693/?t=727 13:58 A Quiet Trace - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22693/?t=838
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Courage and Fear
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on courage as something distinct from general effort, and as a practice that is often shadowed by fear. He traces how fear narrows attention, distorts cognition, and makes letting go feel unsafe, then turns toward the way spiritual practice asks for honesty, surrender, and the willingness to meet uncertainty. The talk closes by connecting courage to virtue, the Buddhist perfections, and a quiet question about what love looks like when it is less afraid.
Chapters: 00:13 Opening to courage - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=13 01:40 Fear at the threshold - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=100 03:58 Faith and the path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=238 06:08 How fear works - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=368 08:25 Groundless moments - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=505 10:03 Terror and waiting - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=603 11:34 Fear of suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=694 13:56 Shared courage - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=836 15:30 Love less afraid - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22672/?t=930

item.title: Dharmette: Surrender
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on surrender as a quiet but central part of practice: not resignation, but a willing release of control, habit, and the need to know. He moves from Leonard Cohen and St. Augustine into a Buddhist account of mindfulness, samadhi, and insight as forms of letting the self soften. The talk stays grounded in ordinary practice, where surrender becomes a way to meet experience honestly and find a different kind of power.

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on surrender as a quiet but central part of practice: not resignation, but a willing release of control, habit, and the need to know. He moves from Leonard Cohen and St. Augustine into a Buddhist account of mindfulness, samadhi, and insight as forms of letting the self soften. The talk stays grounded in ordinary practice, where surrender becomes a way to meet experience honestly and find a different kind of power.

item.title: Dharmette: Self-Esteem Preoccupation as Symptom
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on self-esteem as a symptom rather than a solution, drawing on social psychology and Dharma to question the habit of measuring worth. He moves from scientific conversation and familiar retreat anecdotes into a quieter argument that suffering, fear, and need deprivation generate self-preoccupation, and that practice can soften that grip. The talk closes by pointing toward ordinariness, ethical living, refuge, and the possibility that it is enough simply to be a person.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening the conversation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=12 01:26 Science and Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=86 02:29 Self-esteem as symptom - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=149 04:37 Not-self without idealism - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=277 06:23 Ordinary worthiness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=383 07:49 Selfing and pain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=469 10:06 Thawing the wound - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=606 12:07 Ethics and refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=727 14:16 Just being a person - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22667/?t=856

item.title: Dharmette: Expansiveness of Awe
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on awe as a kind of pleasant disorientation that widens experience rather than narrowing it. He moves from nature and virtuosity to moral beauty, the history of the Dharma, and the spaciousness that can appear when suffering and convention are seen more lightly. The talk closes by linking awe with freedom, love, and a less claustrophobic way of living.
Chapters: 00:12 Disorientation and Awe - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=12 01:19 What Awe Does - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=79 02:56 Nature, Skill, Goodness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=176 05:10 Beyond Claustrophobia - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=310 06:37 Dharma Across Time - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=397 08:33 Closeness of Peace - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=513 10:56 Freedom and Convention - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=656 13:24 Stars and Smallness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=804 15:32 Living in Awe - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22664/?t=932

item.title: Dharmette: Grief as a Facet of Dharma
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers grief as something deeper than a passing emotion: a companion to wakefulness, love, and ethical growth. He moves through the Buddha’s life, the First Noble Truth, and contemporary psychology to suggest that Dharma practice does not erase grief so much as transmute it. The talk stays close to the lived experience of change, loss, and the widening of the heart.

item.title: Dharmette: Humor in the Dharma
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a short Dharma talk on humor and playfulness as part of practice, arguing that seriousness is not the same as sincerity and that insight can make room for laughter. He moves through personal stories, references to Thai Forest teachers, and quotes from George Saunders, Freud, Nietzsche, and Winnicott to show how humor can reveal truth, loosen self-consciousness, and deepen friendship. The talk closes with an account of crying and laughing at once, suggesting that grief, joy, and peace can co-occur without canceling each other out.
Chapters: 00:11 A playful fake list - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22653/?t=11 01:28 Captain Buzzkill - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22653/?t=88 03:54 Seriousness and lightness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22653/?t=234 06:08 Truth told quickly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22653/?t=368 07:37 Learning to play - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22653/?t=457 09:02 Therapy and play - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22653/?t=542 10:38 Playing with self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22653/?t=638 12:43 Crying and laughing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22653/?t=763

item.title: Dharmette: Question about Etiquette Approaching Teachers
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver responds to a listener’s question about etiquette in approaching meditation teachers by shifting the focus from rules to relationship. He frames teaching as service and devotion, then explores projection, transference, and the ways teachers and students can use contact to see the mind more clearly. The talk closes with a careful distinction between wanting to be loved and wanting to be understood, and with sincerity as the heart of genuine Dharma exchange.

item.title: Talk: Spiritual Friendship and Sila as Our Practice
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how practice is shaped not just by private effort, but by spiritual friendship, Sangha, and the ways other people enter the mind. He moves from the Buddha’s teaching that spiritual friendship is the whole of the holy life into a close exploration of sila: ethical conduct as embodied care, self-respect, and a life with less regret. The talk also turns toward retreat, moral sanity in a difficult world, and the quiet ways relationship helps us see ourselves more clearly.

item.title: Evening Reflections
item.description:
In this short evening reflection, Matthew Brensilver invites a simple shift of orientation: to appreciate one’s own goodness without turning that into self-indulgence. He explores the tension around self-love, deserving, shame, and pride, and then points toward a more basic sense of worthiness that comes before those judgments. The talk closes by encouraging that felt sense of goodness to accompany the listener into the night, sleep, and dreams.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Talk: Knowing, Not-Knowing, Selfing
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores Dharma as a disciplined movement between knowing and not knowing: learning enough to see suffering clearly, while loosening the need to control experience with certainty. He connects self-knowledge to dialogue, sangha, and the willingness to let familiar identity become less solid. The talk gradually shifts from ideas about practice into a close examination of how selfing is built from habit, feeling tone, and subtle assumptions.

item.title: Evening Reflections - A Poem
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reads and reflects on a poem about impermanence, using a mountain retreat in New Mexico as the setting for a quiet confrontation with time, fragility, and change. The talk moves from the feel of austere landscape to a direct meditation on mortality, then toward a more spacious relationship to fear and grief. It ends with a simple emphasis on remembering, letting go, and allowing the warmth that can come with loss.

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver reads and reflects on a poem about impermanence, using a mountain retreat in New Mexico as the setting for a quiet confrontation with time, fragility, and change. The talk moves from the feel of austere landscape to a direct meditation on mortality, then toward a more spacious relationship to fear and grief. It ends with a simple emphasis on remembering, letting go, and allowing the warmth that can come with loss.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Talk: Whys and Hows of Effort - Energy & Bhava Tanha
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on effort as something steadied by trust, patience, and a clear sense of why we practice. He moves from the hidden way goodness accrues, to motivation rooted in benefit for others, to the tension between wholesome effort and craving for becoming. The talk closes by warning against spiritual striving and inviting a more receptive, willing relationship to practice.
Chapters: 00:00 Listening and trust - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22618/?t=0 05:44 Practice that simply continues - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22618/?t=344 12:21 Why make the effort - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22618/?t=741 17:00 Precious human life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22618/?t=1020 19:00 Against the stream - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22618/?t=1140 32:07 Open palm of release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22618/?t=1927 40:08 Non-gaining practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22618/?t=2408 45:55 Your own path - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22618/?t=2755

item.title: Dharmette: Fear at the Gateway of Stillness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver responds to a question about fear that arises as meditation deepens and stillness begins to gather. He describes why letting go can feel unsafe, how vigilance and self-protection become fused with consciousness, and why the edge of practice can bring spiritual vertigo. The talk moves toward practical ways of meeting fear: knowing it clearly, softening it with love, acclimatizing to mortality and change, and trusting the silence rather than resisting it.
Chapters: 00:17 The question of fear - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=17 01:46 Safety and vigilance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=106 03:00 Why letting go feels unsafe - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=180 05:21 At the gateway - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=321 06:59 Spiritual vertigo - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=419 09:31 Working with fear - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=571 11:32 Training the nervous system - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=692 13:28 Mortality and faith - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=808 15:03 Surrender to silence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-22489/?t=903

item.title: Dharmette: Moral outrage and love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how deep care can become entangled with outrage, disgust, and the urge to punish. He asks how moral commitment can stay clear and useful without hardening into hatred, drawing on Ruth King and Hannah Chapman along the way. The talk moves toward grief, fear, and the idea that love—not anger—has to have the last word.
