item.title: Overconfidence As A Defilement
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores overconfidence as a spiritual and psychological problem, linking it to attachment to views, self-deception, and the ways certainty hardens into harm. He moves from Buddhist teachings on anicca and delusion into moral uncertainty, the limits of knowing, and the need for humility in personal and public life. The talk closes by turning toward embodied awareness and a careful relationship to feeling, where care can remain open without being captured by clinging.

item.title: Brian Lesage, Cara Lai, Matthew Brensilver: Closing Session (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
This closing session moves from a guided landing into the body, to a small-group speaking practice, and then into reflections on how retreat practice carries into daily life. The teaching emphasizes gradual change, sensitive integration, ethical clarity, and the need to meet transition without forcing it. The session ends with a communal closing, forgiveness, and a blessing for the goodness cultivated together.

item.title: Language is the hammer and every problem, a nail (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how much of meditation practice is shaped by the mind’s ongoing attempt to manage experience, interpret feeling, and solve the problem of self. He moves from vedana and restlessness into the limits of language, the habit of narrating life, and the relief that can come from not immediately turning every sensation into a project. The talk gradually opens toward acceptance, awareness, and the idea that some problems need solving while others need to dissolve.

item.title: Dharmette: Coming Home and Leaving Home
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a movement between coming home and leaving home: returning to what the heart already knows, then learning to love new things. He explains that transformation usually happens gradually, through small repeated gestures rather than a single decision, and uses Plato, Alfred Adler, and a cited philosopher to make that case. The talk ends by returning to the image of a “new home,” where practice slowly reshapes what feels familiar and worth loving.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Fear and Language
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores the uneasy link between fear and language, suggesting that much of thinking is not just a cause of suffering but also a response to it. He moves from the value of words, to the mind’s urge to reduce uncertainty, to the way clinging, comparison, and selfing are organized through language. The talk closes by pointing toward a quieter trust: letting go, receiving phenomena as invitations to release, and placing faith in silence and awareness.
Chapters: 00:09 Words and nourishment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=9 01:23 Silence and language - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=83 03:06 Fear beneath thought - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=186 04:50 Anicca and uncertainty - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=290 07:14 Clinging and self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=434 09:37 No problems - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=577 11:31 Trusting release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=691 13:36 Closing blessings - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20282/?t=816

item.title: Dharmette: Reflections on a RJ Davidson Quote
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short passage from R.J. Davidson and colleagues to unpack what mindfulness training is said to cultivate: steadier attention, meta-awareness, de-reification, non-reactivity, and a loosening of self-story. He then moves from the language of the research into a more contemplative register, using images of attention, gravitational pull, and event horizons to describe how thought can absorb experience and how practice helps us wake up from that pull. The talk closes by turning to equanimity and the softer, less sticky relationship to self that can emerge when identification relaxes.

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short passage from R.J. Davidson and colleagues to unpack what mindfulness training is said to cultivate: steadier attention, meta-awareness, de-reification, non-reactivity, and a loosening of self-story. He then moves from the language of the research into a more contemplative register, using images of attention, gravitational pull, and event horizons to describe how thought can absorb experience and how practice helps us wake up from that pull. The talk closes by turning to equanimity and the softer, less sticky relationship to self that can emerge when identification relaxes.

item.title: Dharmette: The Clinging Before Clinging
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how clinging can begin long before obvious craving or aversion appears. Drawing on the Dhammapada, dependent origination, and a Zen saying about the disappearing subject, he traces how subtle volitional habits shape perception, selfing, and the feeling that the world has hardened into fixed things. The talk closes by pointing toward relinquishment: a simpler, less comparative way of meeting experience that is both disenchanted and quietly amazed.
Chapters: 00:12 Returning to old loves - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=12 00:48 Mind and idealism - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=48 02:12 Experience is shaped - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=132 03:25 Clinging before clinging - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=205 05:00 Models that never idle - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=300 06:39 Formative energies - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=399 08:03 Making the world solid - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=483 10:44 Relinquishment and simplicity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=644 13:03 Wonder without selfing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20194/?t=783
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Becoming Unintimidated by Suffering
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on what it means to meet suffering without fear, beginning with the experience of listening to pain that is both sacred and ordinary. He traces how pain gets bound up with shame, why being witnessed can be healing, and how genuine compassion depends on becoming less intimidated by our own suffering. The talk closes by returning to the Buddha’s instruction to comprehend dukkha: know it in awareness, and let that understanding open into innocence, confidence, and care for others.
Chapters: 00:09 Meeting pain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=9 02:12 Pain and shame - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=132 05:16 Witnessing without fixing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=316 06:46 Norman Fischer on compassion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=406 08:47 Not missing dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=527 10:30 Awareness and space - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=630 12:17 Innocence and confidence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20160/?t=737
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: New Questions
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on teaching, the shaping force of sangha, and how Dharma learning can depend on being both settled and unsettled. He moves from a personal, self-effacing opening into a meditation on pedagogy, cognitive impasses, and the way familiar understandings crack open into new questions. The talk closes by asking what new language the Dharma gives us for self, love, death, and consolation.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Letting Go (5 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on why Dharma practice can feel risky: it asks us to stop managing, stop over-reading, and enter a more vulnerable relationship with experience. Drawing on William James, Nisargadatta, Akena, and Ajahn Sucitto, he frames letting go as a courageous descent out of the personal world rather than a simple technique for feeling better. The talk moves toward a quiet claim that faith is what allows that descent, and that even a brief moment of trusting awareness can change the heart.
Chapters: 00:09 James and tragic depth - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=9 02:13 What the path offers - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=133 03:31 Trying something new - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=211 04:54 Risk of letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=294 06:47 Power in helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=407 07:55 Effective suffering - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=475 09:08 The rope of release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=548 11:31 Fewer signposts - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=691 13:09 Faith and gratitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-20006/?t=789

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Ourselves (4 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores what it means to have faith in oneself without building up a flattering story of self. He argues that real confidence comes through anatta, the lessening of self-deception, and a quieter trust in direct knowing, bodily cues, and intuitive discernment. The talk moves from skepticism about self-esteem language to a practical account of how to become more accountable to what we already know.
Chapters: 00:12 Self-esteem revisited - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=12 01:41 Faith beyond story - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=101 03:19 Not-self and confidence - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=199 05:23 Trusting body and mind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=323 06:45 Intuitive knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=405 08:15 Less self-deception - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=495 09:40 Subtle cues and data - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=580 11:14 Accountable to knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19994/?t=674
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Faith in Love (3 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a kind of faith that is not certainty, but trust in love as the deeper response to suffering, aversion, and fear. He opens with a tender poem by Louise Cook, then turns to the psychology of hatred, the necessity of open-heartedness, and the way practice can reveal that deep happiness depends on love. In the latter half, he uses dependent arising and a reference to Martha Nussbaum to show how following emotion all the way through can lead not to hate, but to kindness, compassion, joy, and equanimity.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening Poem - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=12 01:07 Faith and Doubt - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=67 02:02 Love Against Aversion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=122 04:17 Open-Hearted Happiness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=257 06:20 Anger’s Dead End - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=380 07:59 Dependent Arising - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=479 10:27 Emotion and Need - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=627 11:46 Following to Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19985/?t=706
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Emerging From Doubt
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores doubt as a kind of disorientation: the mind keeps reaching for certainty, but the more it grasps for answers, the more it can get tangled in thought, comparison, and self-conscious practice. He then turns toward faith—not as belief in a theory, but as a growing trust in direct experience, patience, and what is actually trustworthy. The talk closes with a practical encouragement to stay open amid intensity and let the path unfold without demanding impossible certainty.
Chapters: 00:12 Opening and Theme - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=12 00:57 Doubt as Disorientation - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=57 03:01 Narratives and Rumination - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=181 05:01 What Practice Cannot Do - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=301 07:38 Precision and Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=458 10:04 Trusting Experience - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=604 10:59 Faith as a Hinge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=659 12:15 Closing Encouragement - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19971/?t=735
Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Faith in Our Dharma Path (1 of 5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on faith as something quieter and sturdier than belief or optimism. He traces his own suspicion of the word, then shows how practice can become a surrender that does not deny pain or uncertainty. The talk moves toward trust in awareness, truth, love, and a sense of belonging in the Dharma rather than in borrowed views.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Not-self and self expression
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores not-self as a practical loosening of performance, shame, and fixed identity. Starting from a literary image of masks, he moves through social presentation, self-hatred, and the pressure of self-view, then turns toward flexibility, relational trust, and a more embodied sense of being real. The talk argues that insight into anatta does not erase personality; it frees goodness, weirdness, and sincerity to show up without clinging.

item.title: Mindfulness, Mind-wandering, and Equanimity
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver frames mindfulness as a practical training in learning what the mind does, rather than a quest for silence or special states. He moves from the pull of mind-wandering and self-referential thought to the role of sustained attention, de-reification, and non-reactivity, and then broadens into equanimity as the quality that lets suffering crest and soften. The talk closes by showing how this wider, steadier relationship to experience reduces identification with story and helps pain become something metabolized rather than spilled onto others.
Chapters: 00:27 Mindfulness as learning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=27 01:53 What we actually want - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=113 04:50 The allure of unconsciousness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=290 08:18 Mind wandering as baseline - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=498 14:11 Continuity without strain - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=851 22:27 Anchoring and noticing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=1347 30:16 Thought, space, and reactivity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=1816 38:20 Equanimity and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=2300 44:29 Less story, more life - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19654/?t=2669

item.title: Dharmette: Compassion & Equanimity
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores compassion and equanimity as a paired practice rather than separate ideals. He moves from the Buddha’s nuance and resistance to fundamentalism, into the willingness to feel suffering vividly, then into a quiet kind of love that can rest without collapsing into apathy or blame. The talk closes by describing an alternating rhythm of love and rest, where each supports the other.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Passivity & Action
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores a common misunderstanding of equanimity: that being receptive or passive means not acting. He argues instead that receptivity can help us see more clearly, choose more wisely, and make our actions more effective, especially when we understand craving, impermanence, and our own limits. The talk also turns toward love, self-knowledge, and how good will changes the way we move through the world and relate to others.
Chapters: 00:12 Equanimity Is Not Apathy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=12 01:25 Passivity in Service - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=85 02:25 Retreat as Experiment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=145 04:30 Action Becomes More Effective - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=270 06:22 Seeing Impermanence Clearly - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=382 08:06 Forecasting and Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=486 10:11 Knowing Our Limits - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=611 12:17 Respecting Others Carefully - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=737 14:24 A Virtuous Cycle - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19426/?t=864

item.title: Dharmette: Disenchantment & Engagement
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how Buddhist practice can hold two movements at once: disenchantment with samsara and a real care for the world. He begins with the vulnerability of human life, turns to the Bahhiya Sutta and the idea of samvega, and then asks how renunciation and engagement fit together in a modern lay path. The talk closes by arguing that letting go of grasping can make room for compassion, not indifference.

item.title: Dharmette: Effort & Effortlessness
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on the tension between effort and effortlessness in Dharma practice, arguing that early training requires energy but that practice cannot be sustained by willpower alone. He describes how effort can tangle with self-view, how habits and the nervous system gradually become allies, and how awareness and wholesome action can become more natural over time. A cited passage from William James helps frame the talk as a movement from strain toward trained ease.

Source: AudioDharma

item.title: Dharmette: Confidence & Humility
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores confidence and humility as complementary qualities in Dharma practice, rather than opposites. He moves from the idea that wisdom is context-sensitive to the ways practice becomes more trustworthy, more ordinary, and less divided from life. The talk ends by linking humility with epistemic caution, nonviolence, and a willingness to keep looking.

item.title: Welcome - Abiding in Care
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver opens with a quiet welcome and then reflects on what practice changes: less project-management of life, less fixation on past and future, and a more organic resting in the present. He uses an overheard line from Gil Fronsdal about ambition and vision to question his own relationship to purpose, then turns toward sangha, care, and the way a Dharma life may be lived into rather than engineered. The talk later becomes more explicitly embodied, drawing on Ilan Moffat’s phrase “becoming available to the Dharma,” and ends by bringing wisdom and love together as two wings of awakening.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening Welcome - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=1 00:47 Life Less Managed - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=47 03:03 Ambition and Vision - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=183 04:44 A Record of Care - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=284 07:23 Living Into Practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=443 10:52 Entering Sangha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=652 17:23 The Form of Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=1043 22:07 Becoming Available - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=1327 31:44 Wisdom and Love - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19366/?t=1904

item.title: Dharmette 2 - Love, Helplessness, Equanimity, and Affect
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver uses early Buddhist passages, modern philosophy, and recent research to look at love as something real, costly, and often inseparable from helplessness. He moves from the Buddha's fear and compassion toward a wider question: how to care without turning care into ego, despair, or a demand that life keep improving. The talk gradually settles into equanimity as a way of staying open at the edge of what we can control, and ends with a vivid reading of Philip Roth on finitude and aliveness.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Setup - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=0 01:11 Fear and the Thorn - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=71 04:27 Love and Helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=267 08:20 Widening Empathy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=500 11:36 Compassion Costs - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=696 14:25 Progress and Rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=865 18:58 Grief and Finitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1138 25:29 Honest Ethical Seeing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1529 31:45 Spending Love Wisely - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1905
Source: AudioDharma

He moves from the Buddha's fear and compassion toward a wider question: how to care without turning care into ego, despair, or a demand that life keep improving. The talk gradually settles into equanimity as a way of staying open at the edge of what we can control, and ends with a vivid reading of Philip Roth on finitude and aliveness.
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Setup - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=0 01:11 Fear and the Thorn - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=71 04:27 Love and Helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=267 08:20 Widening Empathy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=500 11:36 Compassion Costs - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=696 14:25 Progress and Rest - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=865 18:58 Grief and Finitude - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1138 25:29 Honest Ethical Seeing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1529 31:45 Spending Love Wisely - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19372/?t=1905

item.title: Dharmette 1 - Aspects of Love
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores love as more than a feeling: a bodily tone, a state of mind, and a way perception itself gets colored. He moves from the difference between intense and quiet forms of love into how love changes attention, softens self-absorption, and alters what seems prominent or threatening. The talk closes by inviting a simple practice of “ringing the bell” of metta and noticing the reverberations, with the Brahma-viharas framed as a complete set of medicine for experience.
Chapters: 00:01 Love Beyond Feeling - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=1 01:05 Feeling and Mind State - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=65 02:36 Where Love Lives - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=156 04:59 Intensity and Quietness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=299 07:04 Feeling Shapes Consciousness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=424 09:32 Love Infuses the View - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=572 14:00 Space, Bias, and Fear - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=840 17:57 Practice and Return - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19367/?t=1077

item.title: Sila and Our Debt to Others
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on sila as the place where Dharma practice meets other people: speech, action, livelihood, and the practical shape of our debt to one another. He moves from precepts and non-harm into questions of helplessness, grief, and how compassion can become sustainable when balanced by equanimity. The talk widens into civilizational responsibility, then closes with impermanence and the urgency of spending love wisely.

item.title: Intensity, Feeling, Thinking as Coping, Insight
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how retreat can make experience feel more intense rather than less, and how that intensity often gets managed through thinking, story, and explanation. He traces a quiet path through feeling, attention, equanimity, faith, and insight, suggesting that practice is often less about getting somewhere than about meeting what is already here without fighting it. Along the way he weaves in references to Ajahn Sucitto, the Buddha, Sharon Salzberg, Shinzen Young, and others to show how embodied feeling and nonconceptual awareness work together.
Chapters: 00:03 Living dukkha - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=3 01:48 Retreat as intensification - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=108 03:46 Energy and samadhi - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=226 06:57 Naming the intensity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=417 12:43 Feeling at the center - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=763 18:08 Attention and soothing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=1088 24:17 Do nothing practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=1457 31:04 Equanimity and faith - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=1864 38:37 Stories and insight - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-19236/?t=2317

item.title: Duality, Merging, Not-Self and Differentiation in the Interpersonal World (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores how self-acceptance, not-self, and intimacy meet in the relational world, using a retreat exercise of walking toward another person as his central example. He moves from the felt awkwardness of contact into questions of projection, shame, empathy, and the ethical force of another person’s face. The talk then widens into a careful balance between merging and differentiation: how to love without losing clarity, how to care without being consumed, and how equanimity can make compassion more spacious and effective.
Chapters: 00:35 Sitting and acceptance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=35 01:48 Not-self and lucidity - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=108 03:22 Lay life and relationship - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=202 07:55 Approaching another - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=475 11:38 The face and ethics - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=698 17:29 Self in the gaze - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=1049 21:46 Seen as merely seen - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=1306 25:23 Ego, safety, and refuge - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=1523 31:10 Play and feeling real - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-80583/?t=1870

item.title: Knowing and Not-Knowing
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on teaching from the inside of uncertainty, using a retreat-planning story and a cliffside teaching image to make not-knowing the entry point for practice. From there he unfolds a quiet argument that Dharma asks for both confidence and humility, for knowing and for relinquishing the need to know. The talk moves through ignorance, self-study, love, and the role of surrender in insight and samadhi.

item.title: Matthew Brensilver, Randy Fernando: Navigating A.I. from a Buddhist Perspective
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver and Randy Fernando frame AI as a Buddhist problem of attention, suffering, and conditions rather than novelty alone. The talk moves from a brief opening sit, into a sober overview of generative AI and its risks, and then into a Dharma lens on dependent co-arising, sense-making, and the First Noble Truth. It closes with questions about anatta, the future of teaching and practice, and a return to the Brahma Viharas as a steadier ethical orientation.

item.title: The anchor is everywhere
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores meditation not only as a way to stabilize attention, but as a way to reveal the movement of mind itself. He turns from the familiar use of breath as an anchor toward the idea that awareness has no fixed center, so that everything can become part of the field of knowing. The talk closes by returning to non-identification, forgiveness, and the practical instruction to rest where awareness cannot be stopped.
Chapters: 00:00 What an anchor does - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=0 02:03 Balance and transparency - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=123 04:07 What if it's everywhere - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=247 11:19 From circle to field - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=679 12:29 Unpartitioned knowing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=749 20:57 Non-identification - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=1257 23:23 Forgiveness and release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=1403 29:31 Rest where awareness stays - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77833/?t=1771
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Surrender & Faith (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores surrender as a practical part of Dharma rather than a passive giving up. He begins with the feeling of always adjusting life’s knobs, then contrasts willfulness with willingness, and moves into how retreat can reveal helplessness, faith, and the limits of control. Along the way he draws on Augustine, Ajahn Sucitto, William James, Emerson, and Ajahn Chah to show how letting go can make room for insight, mindfulness, and wisdom.
Chapters: 00:00 The urge to control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=0 02:37 A Christian contrast - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=157 04:06 Surrender in Dharma - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=246 09:45 Conditioning and effort - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=585 17:35 Helplessness and faith - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=1055 24:50 Trusting the descent - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=1490 31:58 Mindfulness as release - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=1918 38:28 Opening to change - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-77831/?t=2308
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Cycling through All Four Divine Abodes
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver offers a guided exploration of the four Brahma-viharas as a cycle of related kinds of love: loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity. The talk moves from recognizing who most needs metta, to meeting suffering without burnout, to rejoicing in ordinary goodness, and finally to a quiet, spacious love that can remain present when life cannot be controlled. He emphasizes that these practices support one another, and that even when one quality is missing, another may be the right doorway.

item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
item.itunes:summary:
Matthew Brensilver offers a guided exploration of the four Brahma-viharas as a cycle of related kinds of love: loving-kindness, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity. The talk moves from recognizing who most needs metta, to meeting suffering without burnout, to rejoicing in ordinary goodness, and finally to a quiet, spacious love that can remain present when life cannot be controlled. He emphasizes that these practices support one another, and that even when one quality is missing, another may be the right doorway.

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality - Fear, Helplessness & Self (4/5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores mortality as a way to clarify value, loosen willfulness, and look directly at helplessness. He moves from the felt loss of control around death into a meditation on how selfing arises from fear, then turns to not-self as a help, though not a perfect consolation, for death and loss. The talk closes with Derek Parfit’s reflection on the “glass tunnel” of selfhood and the possibility of living more openly and with less separation.
Chapters: 00:09 Mortality Clarifies Value - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=9 00:48 Helplessness and Selfing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=48 01:51 Death as Humbling - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=111 03:14 The Mirage of Control - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=194 05:08 Training With Helplessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=308 07:29 Loss and Vastness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=449 08:50 Self as Effect - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=530 10:49 Not-Self and Death - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=649 12:04 Parfit and Open Air - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18168/?t=724
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18168

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality and the Clarification of What Matters (3/5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on how modern life floods us with information while leaving us without a story, and he uses that contrast to ask what keeps us from losing the plot. From there he turns toward mortality as a practical contemplation: not to frighten us, but to clarify what matters, loosen old resentments, and make life feel more complete. The talk moves through retreat experience, a David Whyte story, and an interview with Roland Griffiths to show how death can help expose false refuge and deepen wakefulness.
Chapters: 00:17 Noise and Information - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=17 01:54 Losing the Plot - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=114 04:03 A Life in a Moment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=243 05:09 Remembering Death - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=309 07:48 Wakefulness in Illness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=468 09:25 Honey and Poison - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=565 11:38 Practicing Letting Go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18178/?t=698
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18178

item.title: Dharmette: Mortality - Futurelessness & Samadhi (2/5)
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores mortality as a practical contemplation rather than a grim abstraction, asking how death can reveal the mind’s constant orientation toward the future. He moves from William James and the Buddha to a contemporary scientific framing, then back to lived practice: settling the heart, pruning counterfactual thinking, and resting in what is here now. The talk ends by linking deep surrender with the ordinary task of shaping a Dharma life.
Chapters: 00:12 Death and practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18179/?t=12 01:20 The pull of the future - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18179/?t=80 02:52 A trembling world - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18179/?t=172 04:22 Futurelessness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18179/?t=262 06:02 Only this moment - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18179/?t=362 07:48 Science and samadhi - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18179/?t=468 10:09 Sit like dying - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18179/?t=609 12:15 How to live - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-18179/?t=735
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/18179

item.title: Ill-Will & Metta
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver explores ill-will and metta as two very different ways the heart meets suffering. He moves from the mechanics of aversion—how craving, blame, and anger narrow experience—to the possibility of purification, radical accountability, and a softer, more spacious response. Near the end, he turns toward loving-kindness as a form of non-violence that can hold pain without collapse, and he closes by linking wisdom with love and non-contention.
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the problem - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=1 00:58 The Dharma as a broad remedy - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=58 05:18 Purification and practice - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=318 08:07 What and how - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=487 12:37 Craving shapes the view - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=757 17:40 Mara and the turning inward - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=1060 23:41 The heat of aversion - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=1421 31:53 Facing and owning anger - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=1913 38:30 Metta as antidote - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-17159/?t=2310
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/17159

item.description:

item.title: Wednesday Evening Reflection: Rohini's Enlightenment Poem
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on a line from Rohini’s enlightenment poem: the path is not a line on a map, but a great shining world, enter wherever you like. He uses that image to gently undo the pressure to fit Dharma practice into a fixed shape, suggesting instead that we discover our own belonging within it. The talk closes with an encouragement to affirm one’s efforts, rest well, and continue noticing how apparent walls may become doorways.
Chapters: 03:26 A shining world - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=206 03:54 Not fitting in - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=234 04:36 Enter anywhere - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=276 05:19 Belonging and home - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=319 06:05 Nothing left behind - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=365 06:47 From every direction - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=407 07:25 Affirming effort - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=445 08:05 Rest and continue - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-12548/?t=485
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/12548

item.title: Death and the Poignancy of Life
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on death not as a grim abstraction but as a way to clarify what matters, soften the heart, and loosen the pressure to build a story around the self. He moves through examples from literature, Buddhist teachings, psychology, and personal memory to show how mortality can either harden us into fear or open us toward grief, love, and responsibility. The talk ends by returning to practice as a kind of training in letting go and in making friendship with the mind.
Chapters: 00:23 Opening the topic - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=23 03:07 Writing and dancing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=187 10:03 Birth and mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=603 16:55 Practicing with death - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1015 25:30 Resisting and meaning - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1530 29:40 Finitude and grief - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=1780 36:54 Loss makes life vivid - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=2214 41:27 Fear, identity, and softness - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=2487 50:53 Becoming and letting go - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/dharmaseed-60274/?t=3053
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60274/

item.title: Reflecting On An Ajahn Sucitto Quote
item.description:
Matthew Brensilver reflects on an Ajahn Sucitto teaching about why stopping feels difficult: the self stays busy trying to find ground, while practice asks for a deeper loving acceptance of experience as it is. He moves from busyness and silence to self-comparison, mindfulness, and the way a softer relationship to self can make room for more freedom and less fear. The talk closes with philosophical references and a quiet devotional ending that turns the discussion toward welfare, holding, and non-clinging.
Chapters: 00:34 Ajahn Sucitto's quote - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=34 04:09 Busyness as human condition - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=249 09:10 Solitude and avoidance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=550 12:26 Self, agitation, and comparison - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=746 20:16 Mind and narrative self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1216 25:19 Loving the mess - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1519 33:01 Non-dwelling and mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1981 40:25 Closing blessing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=2425
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9909

He moves from busyness and silence to self-comparison, mindfulness, and the way a softer relationship to self can make room for more freedom and less fear. The talk closes with philosophical references and a quiet devotional ending that turns the discussion toward welfare, holding, and non-clinging.
Chapters: 00:34 Ajahn Sucitto's quote - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=34 04:09 Busyness as human condition - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=249 09:10 Solitude and avoidance - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=550 12:26 Self, agitation, and comparison - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=746 20:16 Mind and narrative self - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1216 25:19 Loving the mess - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1519 33:01 Non-dwelling and mortality - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=1981 40:25 Closing blessing - https://jeffharr.is/brensilver/talks/audiodharma-9909/?t=2425
Source: AudioDharma
Original page: https://www.audiodharma.org/talks/9909

item.title: Compulsive Thinking, Concentration & Equanimity
item.description:
(New York Insight Meditation Center) Meditation practice cultivates a diverse set of attentional, emotional and introspective skills. Central to the practice of mindfulness is the stabilization of attention. But before our attention stabilizes, practice can be overstimulating. This talk will explore the process through which the mind comes to rest. In developing this steadiness, equanimity (the capacity to fully permit the flow of both pleasure and pain) is a vital skill. We will see how concentration and equanimity reinforce each other and support a deeper understanding of ourselves. And how this stability, in turn, makes space for the heart to respond with joy and compassion.
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/55103/
item.itunes:author: Matthew Brensilver
